title: Unvalidated opaque `metadata` object passed through verbatim from untrusted manifest JSON
description: At line 196, `result.metadata = o['metadata'] as Record<string, unknown>` stores the raw parsed JSON value with no field-count limit, key-length cap, or depth cap. A hostile manifest can supply a deeply nested or huge `metadata` object (or keys like `__proto__`, `constructor`, `toString`) that reaches callers who spread or iterate the object. While `JSON.parse` itself prevents classic `__proto__` injection in V8, attacker-controlled keys/values can still cause quadratic iteration, unexpected property shadowing in callers, or accidental feature-flag activation if callers check for specific metadata keys.
code_excerpt: if (o["metadata"] !== undefined) { requireObject(o["metadata"], "manifest.metadata"); result.metadata = o["metadata"] as Record<string, unknown>; }

title: ProducedBy.params stored without sanitisation allows attacker-controlled opaque dict
description: `validateProducedBy` calls `requireObject` on `params` (verifies it is a non-array object) but then assigns it verbatim to `result.params`. Like the `metadata` field, this lets a hostile manifest embed arbitrary deeply-nested structure or prototype-adjacent keys. The params object is stored as `Record<string, unknown>` and is later accessible via `manifest.producedBy.params`, which callers may iterate or spread without further checks.

title: Level numeric fields (w, h, bytes, tileSize, cols, rows) have no upper-bound checks, enabling OOM via crafted manifest
description: The validator verifies that `w`, `h`, `bytes`, `tileSize`, `cols`, and `rows` are finite numbers but places no cap on their magnitude. A hostile manifest with e.g. `w = 2^40` passes validation and is later used in `createLevelSource` to compute `w * h * bpp`; although `createLevelSource` does apply a 1-GiB cap for the whole-level branch, the tiled branch relies entirely on `parseJxtcHeader` for bounds checking and the manifest-level numbers are not cross-checked before being handed to allocation paths. A sufficiently large `bytes` field (no cap) can also mislead progress-reporting and quota logic.

title: contenthash accepted as any non-empty string with no format constraint, enabling cache-key confusion
description: The `contenthash` field is validated only for non-emptiness (line 93). An attacker controlling the manifest can inject a contenthash containing path separators (`/`, `..`), colons (which are used as delimiters in `makeLevelCacheKey` → `ch:<hash>`), or Unicode, causing cache-key collisions with keys from other levels or other images. The `makeLevelCacheKey` function unconditionally prepends `ch:` without escaping, so a contenthash like `foo:bar` would produce the same key structure as a legitimate composite key.
code_excerpt:
const contenthash = requireString(o["contenthash"], `${path}.contenthash`);
  if (contenthash.length === 0) fail(`${path}.contenthash`, "must not be empty");

title: master.name accepted as arbitrary string with no path-traversal sanitisation
description: The `name` field in `MasterMetadata` is only required to be a string. No check prevents values like `../../etc/passwd` or null bytes. While the current codebase appears to use `name` only for display / logging purposes, it is stored in the manifest and could reach file-system operations in ingest tooling or be reflected in user-visible error messages, creating a latent path-traversal or log-injection vector if callers ever use it to construct filesystem paths.

title: GalleryIndex.next pagination cursor accepted without format validation, enabling open-redirect if used as URL
description: The `next` field of a `GalleryIndex` is validated only as a non-undefined string (via `requireString`). If any consumer uses this value directly as a URL for the next page fetch (e.g. `fetch(index.next)`), a hostile server response can redirect the client to an arbitrary origin (SSRF in Node.js contexts, or cross-origin data exfiltration in the browser). The field name (`next`) and its `GalleryIndex.next?` position strongly suggest it will be used as a pagination URL.

title: ensureIccProfile writes result onto an arbitrary source object via dynamic property assignment without key sanitisation
description: `ensureIccProfile` caches its result by writing `(source as any)['_iccProfile'] = p` directly onto the caller-supplied object. The key `_iccProfile` is hardcoded and harmless by itself, but the pattern of dynamically stamping properties onto arbitrary objects (without Object.defineProperty / non-enumerable) leaks internal state into objects that may be serialised, iterated, or logged. If a future caller passes an object that is later `JSON.stringify`-ed (e.g. a manifest entry used in telemetry), the Promise stub would appear in the output.

title: parseWorkerReply trusts worker-supplied w/h dimensions without upper-bound checks
description: In `parseWorkerReply`, a `decode-reply` with `ok: true` is accepted if `pixels instanceof Uint8Array`, `typeof d.w === 'number'`, and `typeof d.h === 'number'`. There is no cap on `d.w` or `d.h`. A compromised or malicious worker script could reply with `w = 2^30, h = 2^30`, causing `minBytes = w * h * bpp` to overflow to a large positive number (or Infinity), bypassing the `pixels.byteLength < minBytes` guard (line 661-663) and feeding an undersized pixel buffer into the stitch path. In a single-origin web app this is low severity, but in Electron or Node.js where workers load external scripts the impact is higher.

title: Hand-rolled validator duplicates Zod/Valibot pattern without exhaustive numeric-range contracts
description: The entire manifest-validate.ts is a hand-rolled schema validator. While it covers type checks, it omits numeric range contracts (max w/h, max bytes, max level count, max qualityCurve array length) and string format contracts (contenthash hex-only, name no-null-bytes). Replacing or augmenting with a schema library (Zod/Valibot) would centralise these constraints, make them auditable, and prevent future contributors from adding new fields without security-relevant range checks.

title: No evidence of npm audit or Dependabot configuration for transitive dependency vulnerabilities
description: The package imports `@casabio/jxl-wasm` and `@casabio/jxl-scheduler` at runtime and depends on `fast-check` in tests. There is no visible `npm audit` step in CI, no Dependabot/Renovate config, and no `overrides` section in package.json for known-vulnerable transitive deps. A vulnerable transitive dep (e.g. a WASM loader or bundler helper) could silently affect this package.
